titleStmt.title: Letter from Peter McGinn to Robert Chalmers, 20 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Peter McGinn

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Belfast based solicitor Peter McGinn (1864-) to Under-Secretary for Ireland Sir Robert Chalmers (1858–1938). After the Easter Rising Chalmers was sent to Ireland as under-secretary, replacing Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939) who had resigned along with Augustine Birrell (1850-1933), his chief secretary. In the letter, McGinn, who is representing Mr. Jones, enclosed a letter that he wrote to the National Health Insurance Company requesting money which is entitled to Mr Jones. However on each occasion McGinn's letter have gone unanswered, so he has written to Chalmers to see what is to be done about this issue.

msIdentifier.collection: NAI; Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers; CSO RP/1916/8631/1

correspAction.placeName: 151 Donegall Street, Belfast, County Antrim, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
20 May 1916 Dear Sir ,
I enclose a copy of a letter I wrote the
National Health Insce. Comrs. on the 11th April last
I wrote the said Commissioners for a reply on
the 21st April, 3rd May and 12th May, but I
got no acknowledgement of any of my letters.
The Irish Transport and General Workers Union
was a society approved of by the National Health
Insurance Comrs. Although controlled beyond
men as the notorious Larkin and the rebel
Connolly recently shot. James is entitled to
10p per week and is without any means. He
is suffering from consumption and if he had
the money which the law says he is entitled
to it would soften and sooth his suffering.
I hope you will enliven the Natl. Health Insce
Comrs. as to their duties. Are employers still
bound to pay weekly into this Society, and if
they stop doing so will they be prosecuted? Yours faithfully P McGinn The Under Secretary
Dublin Castle
Dublin
Copy 11 Donegal Street,
Belfast , 11th April 1916
re National Health Insce Act
Dear Sir,
Alex. Jones 6 Dock Lane, Belfast has instructed
me to inform you that he gave his contribution
books and stamped cards to an agent in Belfast
of the Irish Transport and General Workers Union
Mr. Jones is a member of that Union. Mr. Jones
has applied several times at their office in
Belfast for his Contribution book, but always
got the reply that his book was at "Libery Hall",
and would be got back for him in a day or two.
I have also applied several times for the book
and got sev similar answers. I also wrote on
Mr. Jones' behalf to the Secretary of the Union of
"Liberty Hall" Dublin but I got no reply from him
I will be obliged if the Commissioners will put
their powers in force and get Mr Jones his
contribution book
Yours faithfully P McGinn The Secy
National Health Insurance Comp
Dublin
